headlines.title: 'Let's Go Fishin" Lure for Many Citizens of Ft. Worth
headlines.subtitle: Twelve Hundred Odd Go Each Week-End and One in Fifty Will Catch Fish, but Rich and Poor Will Be Sunburned and Happy.
headlines.fullText:
"Let's go fishin'."
Just about twelve hundred and fourteen citizens of Fort Worth will say that Saturday afternoon, and just about twelve hundred and fourteen or more citizens will say, "Yes, let's," and that's why Sunday will find the banks of the Trinity, Clear Fork, Lake Worth and a dozen or more other streams and small lakes fringed with a fishing population.
It isn't only the small boy who goes fishing. In fact the small boy will be in the minority. There will be middle-aged men and, yes, women, on the banks- industrious is the word, for the twelve hundred and fourteen or more citizens of Fort Worth will Sunday afternoon sit huddled up in a sweltering sun and bait and rebait hooks, and sweat, and keep a watchful eye on corks that won't bob and a few that do, and that's all.
Won't All Catch Fish.
No, they won't all catch fish. Not one in 100 will catch more than a wiggling minnow or two, but they will come home sunburned, limb-weary and there won't be enough fish among the whole bunch to bring 10 cents at the highest market price and they'll raise their hands and swear they won't do it again-but they'll all be in line by the next Saturday night.
It isn't just the working people, either, who will be found hunched up on burning rocks and hot sand, fighting the gnats and putting wiggly worms on hooks for many automobiles will be lined up around Lake Worth before Sunday, they will be there next Sunday and there'll be more every Sunday as the summer gets older.
Beginning to Bite.
Fishing isn't good just yet, but the catfish are beginning to bite freely as the waters clear after the floods and occasionally a white perch is being caught, and even more occasionally a black or white bass. And one black bass in the hands of a fellow citizen about the week-end would start a third of the citizens out of almost any block in the city out on "their own."
Most of the twelve hundred and fourteen who suggest the fishing trips don't know anything about it except that you tie a hook onto a string, and tie the string onto a pole and put some kind of bait on the hook and throw it in the water. Four out of five don't know that the hook they are using is three times too big for the kind of fish they are after, and that they have not a ghost of a chance to get fish in the places they choose because they happen to be shady. But if all the twelve hundred and fourteen fishermen and fisherwomen with their guests knew just the right kind of hooks and lines and bait to use, and where and when to use them, it would be too bad for the fish around.
Fifty "Lucky."
So about one out of fifty of all the fishermen who go out Sunday morning will easily and quietly bring home a nice string of catfish and perch, with a bass or two, and the others will talk about "never having any luck." If they used just the same methods as the fellow who has all the luck, they would all have fish for awhile, until the fish ran out.
There are plenty of places to go fishing about Fort Worth, plenty where there are fish, for with the government stocking Lake Worth and the water being let out into the streams because of the dam, fish have multiplied in the streams. There are a great many carp in Lake Worth and the older fishermen kill these or throw them back.
But even the carp are enough to bring squeals of delight from the automobile fishermen who go equipped mostly with ice cream clothes, high collars and patent leather pumps. They furnish plenty of amusement for children, but along with the gar fish with which the lake abounds they are far from being desired.

headlines.title: Indian Girl Who Fired School Sent to Reformatory
headlines.fullText: MUSKOGEE, Okla., June 19.- Louisa McGuire, a Chickasaw Indian girl who burned the main building of the Chickasaw National Seminary for Indian girls at Bloomfield because she wanted to go home, and did not know how else to accomplish it, will have to go to another reform school. She was an unwilling student in the spring when the buds commenced to swell and the aroma of growing things sent the call to the woods and the river where she had lived all her life, so that she could not resist. She could not escape the school, so she tied all her clothing in a bundle, secreted it in the attic and set the building on fire. Many of the girls had narrow escapes in getting out. Suspicion was directed to the girl and she confessed. At the present term of the Federal court, having no other place to send her, the girl was sentenced to the reform school and will be sent to the Chillicothe institute under contract.

headlines.title: OPIUM CONVENTION TO BECOME OPERATIVE
headlines.subtitle: Agreement Reached Whereby Plan of Suppression Can Be Put in Effect Without All Signing.
headlines.fullText: THE HAGUE, June 19.-The third international opium conference has reached an agreement that the international convention looking to the suppression of the opium traffic, drafted by the conference in 1912, can come into operation as soon as the adhering powers have ratified it; despite the abstention of some of the powers. This convention aims not only at the suppression of the opium traffic, but other perilous habit forming drugs. Thirty-four nations have taken part in the deliberations on this subject, although not all of the states have signed the convention.

headlines.title: EXTRA PAY BARRED IN RESERVE BANKS
headlines.subtitle: Officials Who Expect to Be Appointed Are Concerned Over Federal Law.
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
WASHINGTON, June 19.- Officials of banks that have signified their intention of becoming a member of the Federal reserve system are writing to the treasury department seeking an interpretation of Section 22 of the Federal reserve act, which provides that officers of member banks shall not receive any compensation except their regular salary for any transaction of business of the bank.
Comptroller of the Currency Williams refused to discuss the provision further than to say:
"It is designed to prevent practices which have been very pernicious." The comptroller made it clear that he does not care to interpret the act in detail, as that duty will fall upon the Federal reserve board after it comes into existence.
The paragraph which has caused so much comment in banking circles reads:
"Other than the usual salary or directors' fee paid to any officer, director or employe of a member bank and other than a reasonable fee paid by said bank to such officer, director or employe for service rendered to such bank, no officer, director, employe or attorney of a member bank shall be a beneficiary of, or receive directly or indirectly any fee, commission, gift or other consideration in connection with any transaction or business of the bank."
The penalty for violating this section is a fine not to exceed $5,000, imprisonment for not more than one year, or both.

headlines.title: Chicago's White Suit Crusade Is Draped in Black
headlines.fullText:
Special to The Star-Telegram.
CHICAGO, June 19.- Chicago was flabbergasted by the inauguration of the "white clothes for men" crusade. That was in the morning when the suburban trains and street cars began to disgorge their loads into the city. What Chicago and its soot-laden atmosphere did to the suits of white linen or flannel, white shirts, white panama hats, white ties and white shoes before the wearers got home at night is another story.
The crusade was planned among the occupants of some of the downtown office buildings during the hot spell of last week, but the response was very meager today, but the start was made. The effect was startling, at least until the soot got in its work and then all colors look alike.

headlines.title: BRANDON THOMAS DIES
headlines.subtitle: Author of "Charley's Aunt" Toured America in 1885.
headlines.fullText: LONDON, June 19.-Brandon Thomas, actor and playwright, died here today, aged 58. He was best known as the author of "Charley's Aunt," a play which was performed many thousands of times in all parts of the world. Thomas studied civil engineering, but left that profession in 1879 to become an actor. He was on the stage for twenty-eight years. He toured America in 1885. While still on the stage he wrote many comedies and was also the author of several popular songs.

headlines.title: Abusive Language Charged.
headlines.fullText: Ellis Cooper, 1321 North Commerce, appeared in police court Friday morning on a charge of using abusive language against C. Seeger, 1619 Lincoln. The trial was postponed until Monday, as Cooper's daughter is a witness against him.

headlines.title: RAILROAD MAN DIES; RUN OVER BY TRAIN
headlines.subtitle: Bob Thorpe of Paris, Formerly Employed on Sugarland State Road, Expires in Hospital.
headlines.fullText:
A man giving his name as Bob Thorpe, and home as Paris, Texas, died in the City-County hospital at 1 a. m. Friday as the result of being run over by a Fort Worth & Denver train. Both legs were crushed so badly that surgeons could do nothing for him.
Checks found in Thorpe's pockets indicated that he was a railroad conductor. His last employment is thought to have been on the state railroad at Sugarland, as a check from there was dated Jan. 1, 1914. Cards also show that he was employed on different Alabama lines.
The body is being held in the morgue of the Fort Worth Undertaking Company. News of his death has been wired to a sister, Mrs. D. D. Noe, R. F. D. No. 2, at Tuscumbia, Ala. Her other name was not known. He was about 40 years, and well dressed, but his pockets contained no money.

headlines.title: SIX BIG COMPANIES PLAN TO USE CANAL
headlines.subtitle: American Firm Will Operate Fleet of Ten Vessels on Panama
headlines.fullText:
WASHINGTON, June 19.- Six established steamship lines already have served notice on the Panama canal management of their intention to make general use of the waterway.
An American steamship company now operating four steamers on the Pacific side and six on the Atlantic between San Francisco and Hawaii has notified the canal that it will merge all ten vessels into one fleet to carry freight in unbroken cargoes from Hawaii to the Atlantic coast as soon as Governor Goethals gives the word that regular steamships may use the waterway.
